Make sure X11 forwarding is enabled in the SSH session settings.
In the server terminal, run:

echo $DISPLAY
xclock
If a clock window opens, X11 is working. You can then try:
fastqc


6.5.2 Windows: VcXsrv alternative
If you use VcXsrv instead of MobaXterm:

Install VcXsrv.
Open XLaunch.
Choose:

Multiple windows
Start no client
Clipboard enabled
Disable access control
PowerShell uses this syntax:
$env:DISPLAY = "127.0.0.1:0.0"
echo $env:DISPLAY
ssh -Y username@SERVER_IP -i "C:\path\to\key.pem"
Inside the SSH session:
echo $DISPLAY
xclock
fastqc






PowerShell is not CMD



Do not use set DISPLAY=... in PowerShell. Use $env:DISPLAY = "127.0.0.1:0.0".




6.5.3 macOS: XQuartz
macOS requires XQuartz for X11.

Install XQuartz from https://www.xquartz.org/.
Open XQuartz once.
Close and reopen Terminal.
Connect with trusted X11 forwarding:

ssh -Y username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem
Test:
echo $DISPLAY
xclock
fastqc
If the window does not appear, make sure XQuartz is running. If X11 is slow, use VNC instead.

title: Software setup before the workshop
text:
6.6 VNC access
VNC opens a full remote desktop. It is often more stable than X11 for graphical tools.






Use VNC only through SSH tunnel



Do not connect directly to a public VNC port. Start VNC on the server, create an SSH tunnel, then connect your VNC client to localhost.





Placeholder: VNC through SSH tunnel concept



6.6.1 Start VNC on the server
First, SSH to the server. Then run:
start-vnc
The first time, it asks you to create a VNC password.
Important:

this is a separate VNC password,
it is not your Linux password,
it is not your private key,
when asked for a view-only password, type n.

The command prints something like:
Display: :2
VNC port: 5902
Write down the VNC port. The port may be 5901, 5902, 5903, or another number.


6.6.2 Generic SSH tunnel
Open a new terminal window on your laptop and keep your original SSH session open. Replace 5902 with the port printed by start-vnc:
ssh -L 5902:localhost:5902 username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem
Keep this tunnel window open.
Then connect your VNC client to:
localhost:5902
If your VNC port is different, use that number instead.


6.6.3 Windows VNC with MobaXterm
In MobaXterm:

Start VNC on the server with start-vnc.
Open Tunneling > New SSH tunnel.
Choose local port forwarding.
Fill in:

Forwarded port: your VNC port
Remote server: localhost
Remote port: your VNC port
SSH server: SERVER_IP
SSH login: username
SSH port: 22
Use private key: key.pem
Example if start-vnc reports port 5902:
Forwarded port: 5902
Remote server: localhost
Remote port: 5902
SSH server: SERVER_IP
SSH login: username
SSH port: 22
Use private key: key.pem
Start the tunnel and keep it running.
Then create a VNC session:
Remote hostname: localhost
Port: your VNC port
Enter the VNC password you created with start-vnc.


6.6.4 macOS VNC
macOS includes Screen Sharing.

Start VNC on the server with start-vnc.
Open a new Terminal window and create the SSH tunnel:

ssh -L 5902:localhost:5902 username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem

In Finder, choose:

Go > Connect to Server

Enter:

vnc://localhost:5902

Enter the VNC password you created with start-vnc.

You can also use RealVNC Viewer or TigerVNC Viewer if Screen Sharing does not work well on your Mac.


6.6.5 Stop VNC when finished
List your VNC sessions:
list-vnc
If the display is :2, stop it with:
stop-vnc 2
If the display is :1, stop it with:
stop-vnc 1
If the remote desktop is locked, run:
fix-vnc-lock
list-vnc
Then restart your own VNC display.

title: Software setup before the workshop
text:
7.1 Windows PowerShell
python --version
pip --version
java -version
ssh -V

title: Software setup before the workshop
text:
7.2 R console
R.version.string
sapply(c("dplyr", "ggplot2", "phyloseq", "vegan"), requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE)

title: Software setup before the workshop
text:
7.3 macOS Terminal
python3 --version
R --version
java -version
ssh -V

If Python, R, SSH, and the R package check work, your laptop is ready for the core workshop activities.

title: Software setup before the workshop
section: 8.1 python is not recognized on Windows
text:
8.1 python is not recognized on Windows
Likely cause: Python was installed without adding it to PATH.
Simple fix:

Open the Python installer again.
Choose Modify or reinstall Python.
Tick Add python.exe to PATH if shown.
Close and reopen PowerShell.
Try:

python --version
Ask an instructor if Windows still cannot find Python.

title: Software setup before the workshop
text:
8.2 pip is not recognized
Likely cause: Windows can find Python but not the separate pip command.
Simple fix: use python -m pip instead:
python -m pip --version
python -m pip install numpy pandas
Ask for help if python -m pip also fails.

title: Software setup before the workshop
text:
8.3 R package installation fails
Likely causes:

internet connection problem,
CRAN mirror problem,
missing system build tools,
or an older package binary is not available.

Simple fixes:

Try again with a different CRAN mirror.
Restart R or RStudio.
On macOS, install Xcode Command Line Tools:

xcode-select --install
Ask an instructor if the error mentions compilers, Fortran, gfortran, or unavailable binary packages.

title: Software setup before the workshop
text:
8.4 phyloseq installation fails
Likely cause: phyloseq comes from Bioconductor, not ordinary CRAN.
Simple fix:
install.packages("BiocManager")
BiocManager::install("phyloseq", ask = FALSE, update = TRUE)
Ask an instructor if Bioconductor says your R version is not compatible.

title: Software setup before the workshop
section: 8.5 macOS asks for command line tools
text:
8.5 macOS asks for command line tools
Likely cause: an R package needs to compile code.
Simple fix:
xcode-select --install
Wait for the installer to finish, then reopen R or RStudio.

title: Software setup before the workshop
section: 8.6 VS Code cannot connect to the server
text:
8.6 VS Code cannot connect to the server
Likely causes:

wrong server IP,
wrong username,
wrong key path,
key file was moved,
server not ready,
or local network blocks SSH.

Simple checks:

Try the same SSH command in PowerShell or Terminal.
Check that the key file exists at the path in your SSH config.
Check that HostName, User, and IdentityFile are correct.

Ask the admin if ordinary SSH fails too.

title: Software setup before the workshop
text:
8.7 SSH says Permission denied
Likely causes:

wrong username,
wrong private key,
private key not selected,
or the admin has not added your account yet.

Simple fix: check the command carefully:
ssh username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem
Ask the admin if the username, server IP, and key are correct but the error continues.

title: Software setup before the workshop
text:
8.8 SSH says Bad permissions
This is common on macOS when the key file is readable by other users.
Fix:
chmod 600 /path/to/key.pem
Then connect again.

title: Software setup before the workshop
text:
8.9 PEM key path contains spaces
On Windows, put quotation marks around the key path:
ssh username@SERVER_IP -i "C:\Users\Your Name\Downloads\microgen2026.pem"
On macOS, either quote the path or move the key to a simpler location:
ssh username@SERVER_IP -i "/Users/Your Name/Downloads/microgen2026.pem"

title: Software setup before the workshop
text:
8.10 MobaXterm cannot open GUI windows
Likely causes:

the MobaXterm X server is not running,
X11 forwarding is not enabled,
or the remote program cannot find DISPLAY.

Simple checks inside the server SSH session:
echo $DISPLAY
xclock
If xclock does not open, use VNC or ask an instructor to check the MobaXterm session settings.

title: Software setup before the workshop
text:
8.11 X11 forwarding does not work
Likely causes:

XQuartz is not running on macOS,
VcXsrv is not running on Windows,
the SSH session was opened without -Y,
or network latency makes X11 unstable.

Simple fix on macOS:
ssh -Y username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem
If it remains unreliable, use VNC.

title: Software setup before the workshop
section: 8.12 VNC tunnel connects but the screen is blank
text:
8.12 VNC tunnel connects but the screen is blank
Likely causes:

you connected to the wrong port,
the tunnel window was closed,
VNC was not started on the server,
or the remote desktop is locked.

Simple checks on the server:
list-vnc
fix-vnc-lock
If needed, stop your display and start again:
stop-vnc 2
start-vnc
Replace 2 with your actual display number.

title: Software setup before the workshop
text:
8.13 Java is not found
Likely cause: Java was not installed or the terminal has not refreshed its PATH.
Simple fixes:

Install Java 17 from an official JDK provider.
Close and reopen PowerShell or Terminal.
Run:

java -version
Ask for help if Java is installed but still not found.

title: Software setup before the workshop
text:
8.14 RStudio cannot find R
Likely cause: RStudio was installed before R, or R installation did not finish.
Simple fixes:

Install R 4.5.0.
Restart the computer.
Open RStudio again.

If RStudio still cannot find R, ask an instructor to check the R installation path.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
4.1 Install Visual Studio Code

Open the official VS Code download page: https://code.visualstudio.com/download
Under Windows, download either:

User Installer x64 if you are using your own laptop and do not have administrator rights, or
System Installer x64 if your administrator or IT team prefers system-wide installation.

Run the installer.
When the installer shows optional tasks, tick these options if available:

Add to PATH
Add “Open with Code” action
Register Code as an editor for supported file types

Finish the installation and open VS Code.

In VS Code, open the Extensions view and install:

Remote - SSH by Microsoft
Python by Microsoft
R by REditorSupport
Quarto by Quarto








What this does



VS Code is the main editor for the workshop. The Remote - SSH extension lets VS Code open your assigned server workspace as if it were a local folder.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
4.2 Install Python 3.13

Open the official Python 3.13 download page: https://www.python.org/downloads/latest/python3.13/
Download the Windows installer (64-bit).
Run the installer.
On the first installer screen, tick Add python.exe to PATH.
Choose Install Now.








Important Windows step



The Add python.exe to PATH checkbox is important. It lets Windows find Python when you type python in PowerShell.


Open PowerShell from the Start menu and check Python:
python --version
pip --version
You should see Python 3.13 and a pip version. pip is the tool Python uses to install Python packages.
Update pip:
python -m pip install --upgrade pip
This asks Python to update its package installer.
Install the required Python packages:
python -m pip install numpy pandas
This installs numpy and pandas, which are common Python packages for numerical and table-based data work.
Verify the packages:
python -c "import numpy, pandas; print('Python packages OK')"
If the packages are installed correctly, PowerShell prints Python packages OK.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
4.3 Install R 4.5.0
R 4.5.0 is the R version used by the Day 4 practical materials. It is no longer the newest R release, so use the official CRAN old-release page for Windows.

Open the official CRAN Windows R 4.5.0 page: https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/base/old/4.5.0/
Download R-4.5.0-win.exe.
Run the installer.
Accept the default options unless your instructor tells you otherwise.

Open R or RGui from the Start menu and run:
R.version.string
You should see a line beginning with R version 4.5.0.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
4.4 Install Rtools45 - only if needed
Most Windows users do not need Rtools at the start. R normally installs pre-built Windows package binaries from CRAN and Bioconductor.
Rtools is needed only when R says it must install a package from source or when an error mentions compilers, make, gcc, g++, gfortran, or Rtools.
For R 4.5.0 on Windows, use Rtools45.






Do not install a different Rtools version



Rtools must match your R version. For this workshop, Windows participants installing R 4.5.0 should use Rtools45, not Rtools44, Rtools43, or older versions.



Open the official CRAN Rtools page: https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/
Choose RTools 4.5.
Download the Rtools45 installer.
Run the installer.
Keep the default installation location, usually:

C:\rtools45






Beginner recommendation



If your R package installation works, you can skip Rtools. If installation fails with a compiler-related message, install Rtools45 and then retry the R package command.


After installing Rtools45, open a fresh R or RStudio session and run:
install.packages("pkgbuild")
pkgbuild::has_rtools(debug = TRUE)
If Rtools is detected, this should return TRUE.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
4.5 Install RStudio Desktop - optional
RStudio is optional. It is helpful if you prefer a graphical interface for R, but VS Code is enough for most workshop tasks.

Open the official Posit download page: https://posit.co/downloads/
Choose RStudio Desktop.
Download the Windows installer.
Run the installer with the default settings.
Open RStudio and check that it starts an R console.







RStudio cannot replace R



RStudio is an editor. R is the programming language. Install R first, then install RStudio.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
4.6 Install R packages
Open R, RGui, or the RStudio Console. Paste this code and press Enter:
install.packages(c(
  "tidyverse",
  "dplyr",
  "tidyr",
  "readr",
  "ggplot2",
  "ggrepel",
  "vegan",
  "patchwork",
  "scales",
  "data.table",
  "broom",
  "knitr"
))
This installs the packages that come from CRAN.
Now install BiocManager if it is not already installed:
if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("BiocManager")
}
BiocManager is the installer used for Bioconductor packages. Many learners will not have it yet.
Then install phyloseq:
BiocManager::install("phyloseq", ask = FALSE, update = TRUE)
phyloseq comes from Bioconductor, so it is installed after BiocManager is available.






If R asks about a CRAN mirror



Choose a mirror close to you, or choose the cloud mirror if it is listed. Any official CRAN mirror should work.


Verify the R packages:
packages <- c(
  "dplyr", "tidyr", "readr", "ggplot2", "ggrepel",
  "phyloseq", "vegan", "patchwork", "scales",
  "data.table", "broom", "knitr"
)

sapply(packages, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE)
Each package should return TRUE. If one returns FALSE, copy the package name and ask an instructor for help.






If R asks to install from source on Windows



Choose the binary version if R offers one. If R says a package must be installed from source, you may need Rtools45. Install Rtools45 from the section above, restart R or RStudio, and run the package installation again.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
4.7 Install MobaXterm portable
MobaXterm is recommended for Windows participants who need SSH, X11 forwarding, SFTP file browsing, or VNC tunneling. The portable edition does not require administrator installation.

Open the official MobaXterm Home Edition page: https://mobaxterm.mobatek.net/download-home-edition.html
Download the Portable edition.
Extract the ZIP file to a folder you can find later, such as Downloads\MobaXterm.
Open the extracted folder.
Run MobaXterm_Personal_*.exe.



4.7.1 Create an SSH session in MobaXterm

Open MobaXterm.
Click Session.
Choose SSH.
In Remote host, enter the server IP address given by the admin.
Tick Specify username and enter your username.
Open Advanced SSH settings.
Tick Use private key and select your .pem file.
Make sure X11-forwarding is enabled.
Click OK to connect.
Save the session if MobaXterm asks.







Keep the key file path simple



If possible, store your .pem key in a folder path without spaces, for example C:\Users\YourName\Downloads\microgen2026.pem. If the path contains spaces, keep quotation marks around it when using PowerShell.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
4.8 Install Java - optional
Java is optional for most participants. It is mainly needed for Artemis. Bandage is usually provided as a standalone app and does not normally require Java.
Use a stable official JDK distribution, such as:

Eclipse Temurin: https://adoptium.net/
Microsoft Build of OpenJDK: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/java/openjdk/download

For the workshop, Java 17 is a good default. Java 11 is also usually compatible with recent Artemis releases.
After installation, open PowerShell:
java -version
You should see a Java version number.


Click to open optional Artemis and Bandage download notes

Artemis official page: https://sanger-pathogens.github.io/Artemis/Artemis/
Artemis GitHub releases: https://github.com/sanger-pathogens/Artemis/releases
Bandage official page: https://rrwick.github.io/Bandage/
Bandage GitHub releases: https://github.com/rrwick/Bandage/releases
For Artemis on Windows, download the Windows release ZIP, extract it, and open artemis.jar after Java is installed.
For Bandage on Windows, download the Windows release ZIP, extract it, and run the Bandage executable. No installation is usually needed.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
5.1 Check chip type

Open the Apple menu in the top-left corner.
Choose About This Mac.
Look for Chip or Processor.

Use this rule:

Processor: Intel means Intel Mac.
Chip: Apple M1/M2/M3/M4 means Apple Silicon Mac.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
5.2 Install Visual Studio Code

Open the official VS Code download page: https://code.visualstudio.com/download
Under Mac, choose:

Intel chip for Intel Macs,
Apple silicon for M1/M2/M3/M4 Macs,
or Universal if you are unsure.

Open the downloaded .dmg file.
Drag Visual Studio Code to the Applications folder.
Open VS Code from Applications.

Install these VS Code extensions:

Remote - SSH by Microsoft
Python by Microsoft
R by REditorSupport
Quarto by Quarto

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
5.3 Install Python 3.13
There are two acceptable routes. Use Route A if you are not comfortable with Terminal.

5.3.1 Route A: official Python installer

Open the official Python 3.13 download page: https://www.python.org/downloads/latest/python3.13/
Download the macOS installer.
Open the .pkg installer.
Follow the default installation steps.

Open Terminal from Applications > Utilities and check Python:
python3 --version
python3 -m pip --version


5.3.2 Route B: Homebrew - optional
Use this only if you already use Homebrew or are comfortable with Terminal.
Install Python 3.13:
brew install python@3.13
This asks Homebrew to install Python 3.13.
Check Python:
python3 --version
python3 -m pip --version
Install Python packages:
python3 -m pip install --upgrade pip
python3 -m pip install numpy pandas
The first command updates the Python package installer. The second installs numpy and pandas.
Verify:
python3 -c "import numpy, pandas; print('Python packages OK')"

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
5.4 Install R 4.5.0
R 4.5.0 is now an older R release. CRAN still provides the official installers in architecture-specific macOS directories.

5.4.1 Intel Mac

Open the CRAN Intel macOS base directory: https://cran.r-project.org/bin/macosx/big-sur-x86_64/base/
Download R-4.5.0-x86_64.pkg.
Open the .pkg file and accept the default installation options.



5.4.2 Apple Silicon Mac

Open the CRAN Apple Silicon macOS base directory: https://cran.r-project.org/bin/macosx/big-sur-arm64/base/
Download R-4.5.0-arm64.pkg.
Open the .pkg file and accept the default installation options.







macOS version note



The R 4.5.0 macOS packages were built for modern macOS versions. If your Mac is older or cannot install the package, ask an instructor before installing a different R version.


Verify in Terminal:
R --version
Or open R.app and run:
R.version.string
You should see R 4.5.0.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
5.5 Install RStudio Desktop - optional

Open the official Posit download page: https://posit.co/downloads/
Download RStudio Desktop for macOS.
Open the downloaded file.
Drag RStudio to Applications if prompted.
Open RStudio and confirm that it detects R.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
5.6 Install R packages
Open R.app or RStudio Console. Paste:
install.packages(c(
  "tidyverse",
  "dplyr",
  "tidyr",
  "readr",
  "ggplot2",
  "ggrepel",
  "vegan",
  "patchwork",
  "scales",
  "data.table",
  "broom",
  "knitr"
))
This installs the packages that come from CRAN.
Now install BiocManager if it is not already installed:
if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("BiocManager")
}
BiocManager is the installer used for Bioconductor packages. Many learners will not have it yet.
Then install phyloseq:
BiocManager::install("phyloseq", ask = FALSE, update = TRUE)
phyloseq comes from Bioconductor, so it is installed after BiocManager is available.
Verify:
packages <- c(
  "dplyr", "tidyr", "readr", "ggplot2", "ggrepel",
  "phyloseq", "vegan", "patchwork", "scales",
  "data.table", "broom", "knitr"
)

sapply(packages, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE)
If R asks whether to install from source, choose the binary option if it is available.






Rtools is not used on macOS



Rtools is for Windows only. On macOS, packages that need compilation use Apple’s Command Line Tools instead.


If compilation fails on macOS, install the Xcode Command Line Tools:
xcode-select --install
This opens an Apple installer for command-line build tools. After it finishes, reopen R or RStudio and try the package installation again.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
5.7 Install Java - optional
Java is optional. It is mainly needed for Artemis.
Use a stable official JDK distribution:

Eclipse Temurin: https://adoptium.net/
Microsoft Build of OpenJDK: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/java/openjdk/download

Choose the correct installer:

Intel Mac: macOS x64
Apple Silicon Mac: macOS aarch64 or Apple Silicon

Verify in Terminal:
java -version


Click to open optional Artemis and Bandage notes for macOS

Artemis is a Java application. Recent Artemis releases require Java 9 or newer, and Java 11 or 17 is a sensible workshop choice.
Download Artemis from the official page:
https://sanger-pathogens.github.io/Artemis/Artemis/
Bandage is not a Java application. Use the official Bandage release page and choose the Mac build for your chip:
https://github.com/rrwick/Bandage/releases
On macOS, if you see a warning that an app is from an unidentified developer, right-click the app and choose Open. If the instructor asks you to clear a quarantine flag for Bandage, use:
xattr -cr /Applications/Bandage.app

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
6.1 Who should use this?
Use this setup if you are comfortable editing VS Code settings and want:

R code completion and IntelliSense,
a better plot viewer inside VS Code,
R workspace, data, help, and plot viewers,
linting warnings while editing R code,
shortcuts for common R operators,
and optional debugging tools.

If you are new to R or VS Code, skip this section for now. You can return to it later.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
6.2 What the advanced setup adds
The VS Code R extension can provide:

R syntax highlighting and code completion,
R terminals inside VS Code,
linting and formatting support,
plot, data, help, and workspace viewers,
R Markdown support,
and optional debugging through a separate R Debugger extension.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 6.3 Step 1: Install R packages used by VS Code

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 6.4 Step 2: Install the correct VS Code extension
text:
6.4 Step 2: Install the correct VS Code extension
In VS Code:

Open Extensions.
Search for R.
Install R by REditorSupport.

Avoid installing several R extensions that do the same job. Multiple overlapping R extensions can cause duplicate commands, conflicting terminals, or confusing error messages.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 6.5 Step 3: Create and use an R terminal
text:
6.5 Step 3: Create and use an R terminal
The official VS Code workflow is to send R code to an R terminal.
Open the Command Palette:

Windows: Ctrl+Shift+P
macOS: Command+Shift+P

Run:
R: Create R terminal
Then open an .R file and press:
Ctrl+Enter
This sends the current line or selected R code to the active R terminal.
To source an entire R file, use:
Ctrl+Shift+S

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 6.6 Step 4: Add recommended VS Code settings
text:
6.6 Step 4: Add recommended VS Code settings
Open VS Code settings JSON:

Open the Command Palette.
Run:

Preferences: Open User Settings (JSON)

Add these settings, merging them with any settings already present:



Click to open suggested VS Code settings for advanced R users

{
  "editor.formatOnType": true,
  "editor.minimap.enabled": false,
  "r.plot.useHttpgd": true,
  "editor.suggest.selectionMode": "whenQuickSuggestion",
  "r.session.useWebServer": true,
  "r.session.data.rowLimit": 50000,
  "r.session.data.pageSize": 250
}
These settings enable httpgd, R session viewer features, and a few useful editor preferences.
Do not replace your whole settings file if it already contains other settings. Merge only the entries you need, and keep valid JSON syntax.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
6.7 Step 5: Optional keybindings
These shortcuts are optional. Add them only if you want familiar R operator shortcuts in VS Code.
Open keyboard shortcuts JSON:

Open the Command Palette.
Run:

Preferences: Open Keyboard Shortcuts (JSON)

Add the bindings below.

Useful shortcuts included:



Shortcut
Inserts




Alt+-
<-


Ctrl+Shift+M
%>%


Ctrl+Shift+C
Toggle line comment


Ctrl+Shift+F10
Kill terminal





Click to open optional R keybindings

[
  {
    "key": "ctrl+shift+c",
    "command": "editor.action.commentLine",
    "when": "editorTextFocus && !editorReadonly"
  },
  {
    "key": "ctrl+/",
    "command": "-editor.action.commentLine",
    "when": "editorTextFocus && !editorReadonly"
  },
  {
    "key": "Ctrl+Shift+m",
    "command": "type",
    "args": { "text": " %>% " },
    "when": "editorTextFocus && editorLangId == r"
  },
  {
    "key": "Alt+-",
    "command": "type",
    "args": { "text": " <- " },
    "when": "editorTextFocus && editorLangId == r"
  },
  {
    "key": "Ctrl+Shift+m",
    "command": "type",
    "args": { "text": " %>% " },
    "when": "editorTextFocus && editorLangId == rmd"
  },
  {
    "key": "Alt+-",
    "command": "type",
    "args": { "text": " <- " },
    "when": "editorTextFocus && editorLangId == rmd"
  },
  {
    "key": "Ctrl+Shift+m",
    "command": "type",
    "args": { "text": " %>% " },
    "when": "editorTextFocus && editorLangId == quarto"
  },
  {
    "key": "Alt+-",
    "command": "type",
    "args": { "text": " <- " },
    "when": "editorTextFocus && editorLangId == quarto"
  },
  {
    "key": "Ctrl+Shift+m",
    "command": "workbench.action.terminal.sendSequence",
    "args": { "text": " %>% " },
    "when": "terminalFocus"
  },
  {
    "key": "Alt+-",
    "command": "workbench.action.terminal.sendSequence",
    "args": { "text": " <- " },
    "when": "terminalFocus"
  },
  {
    "key": "ctrl+shift+f10",
    "command": "workbench.action.terminal.kill"
  }
]

If these shortcuts conflict with shortcuts you already use, remove or edit the conflicting entries. The keybindings are not required for the workshop.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
6.8 Step 6: Optional linting configuration
The VS Code R extension can use the R package lintr for linting. Linting means showing style and code-quality warnings while you edit.
Install lintr in R:
install.packages("lintr")
To reduce distracting style warnings during workshop-style code, create a file named .lintr in your user home folder or project root.
Example Windows location:
C:\Users\YourName\.lintr
Example macOS location:
/Users/YourName/.lintr
Paste this content into .lintr:
linters: linters_with_defaults(
  trailing_blank_lines_linter = NULL,
  line_length_linter = NULL,
  trailing_whitespace_linter = NULL,
  commented_code_linter = NULL,
  indentation_linter = NULL,
  object_usage_linter = NULL,
  object_name_linter = NULL
  )
This turns off several warnings that are often distracting during short practical exercises.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
6.9 Optional radian console
The official VS Code R documentation recommends radian as an optional modern R console. It provides syntax highlighting and a better console experience than the default R terminal.
Install it only if you are comfortable with Python package installation.
Windows:
python -m pip install radian
macOS:
python3 -m pip install radian
After installing, follow the R extension documentation if you want VS Code to use radian as the R terminal. Most workshop exercises do not require radian.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
6.10 Optional R debugging
R debugging in VS Code is provided by the separate R Debugger extension.
Install it only if you need breakpoints and step-through debugging:
https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=RDebugger.r-debugger
Most learners do not need R debugging for the workshop.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
6.11 Quick verification
Create a file called test-r-vscode.R:
x <- rnorm(100)
summary(x)
plot(x)
In VS Code:

Run R: Create R terminal.
Open test-r-vscode.R.
Press Ctrl+Enter on each line.

Expected result:

R code runs in the R terminal.
Completion appears while typing R functions.
The plot appears in the VS Code plot viewer if httpgd is installed and enabled.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 6.12 Advanced R in VS Code troubleshooting
text:
6.12 Advanced R in VS Code troubleshooting

6.12.1 VS Code does not find R
On Windows, reinstall R and make sure the installer saves the R version in the registry if that option appears. This helps the R extension find the R executable automatically.
You can also start R manually in a normal VS Code terminal if the automatic R: Create R terminal command fails.


6.12.2 languageserver fails to install
Likely cause: R needs to compile dependencies.
On Windows, install Rtools45 if the error mentions compilers or source packages. On macOS, install Xcode Command Line Tools:
xcode-select --install
Then restart R and try again.


6.12.3 Plot viewer does not work
Check that httpgd is installed:
requireNamespace("httpgd", quietly = TRUE)
Check that VS Code settings include:
"r.plot.useHttpgd": true
Restart VS Code after changing settings.


6.12.4 Too many lint warnings appear
Use the .lintr configuration above, or temporarily disable linting in VS Code settings.


6.12.5 Keyboard shortcuts conflict with existing shortcuts
Remove or edit the conflicting entries in your keyboard shortcuts JSON. The shortcuts are optional.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 7.1 What information you will receive from the admin
text:
7.1 What information you will receive from the admin
You should receive:

your username,
the server IP address,
a .pem private key file,
and, if relevant, your assigned instance name.







Private key safety



Do not share the .pem key. Do not paste it into this notebook, a chat window, email, or a shared document.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
7.2 SSH from Windows PowerShell
Open PowerShell and replace username, SERVER_IP, and the key path with your real details:
ssh username@SERVER_IP -i "C:\path\to\key.pem"
The quotation marks are important if the path contains spaces.
After login, check your server location:
whoami
pwd
ls -lh /workspace/$USER /scratch/$USER
Use /workspace/<username> for exercises and outputs you want to keep. Use /scratch/<username> for large temporary files.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
7.3 SSH from macOS Terminal
Before your first login, fix the key file permissions:
chmod 600 /path/to/key.pem
This tells macOS that only you can read the key file.
Connect:
ssh username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem
After login:
whoami
pwd
ls -lh /workspace/$USER /scratch/$USER

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
7.4 VS Code Remote SSH


Open VS Code.
Install the Remote - SSH extension.
Open the Command Palette:

Windows: Ctrl+Shift+P
macOS: Command+Shift+P

Choose Remote-SSH: Add New SSH Host.
Paste your SSH command, for example:

ssh username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem

Choose the SSH configuration file suggested by VS Code.
Open the Command Palette again.
Choose Remote-SSH: Connect to Host.
Select your new host.
Open your assigned workspace folder:

/workspace/<username>
Do not use /databases as your main VS Code workspace. Shared database files are usually read-only.

7.4.1 Example SSH config
Windows example:
Host microgen2026
    HostName SERVER_IP
    User username
    IdentityFile C:\path\to\key.pem
macOS example:
Host microgen2026
    HostName SERVER_IP
    User username
    IdentityFile /path/to/key.pem






Long-running commands



If you run a long analysis on the server, use tmux so the command can continue if your network drops:
tmux new -s microgen
Detach with Ctrl-b, then press d. Return later with:
tmux attach -t microgen

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
7.5 X11 forwarding
X11 opens a single graphical program from the server on your laptop. For example, the fastqc window can appear on your own screen while the program runs on the server.

7.5.1 Windows: MobaXterm recommended
MobaXterm includes an X server.

Open MobaXterm.
Check that the X server is running.
Create or open your SSH session.
Make sure X11 forwarding is enabled in the SSH session settings.
In the server terminal, run:

echo $DISPLAY
xclock
If a clock window opens, X11 is working. You can then try:
fastqc


7.5.2 Windows: VcXsrv alternative
If you use VcXsrv instead of MobaXterm:

Install VcXsrv.
Open XLaunch.
Choose:

Multiple windows
Start no client
Clipboard enabled
Disable access control
PowerShell uses this syntax:
$env:DISPLAY = "127.0.0.1:0.0"
echo $env:DISPLAY
ssh -Y username@SERVER_IP -i "C:\path\to\key.pem"
Inside the SSH session:
echo $DISPLAY
xclock
fastqc






PowerShell is not CMD



Do not use set DISPLAY=... in PowerShell. Use $env:DISPLAY = "127.0.0.1:0.0".




7.5.3 macOS: XQuartz
macOS requires XQuartz for X11.

Install XQuartz from https://www.xquartz.org/.
Open XQuartz once.
Close and reopen Terminal.
Connect with trusted X11 forwarding:

ssh -Y username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem
Test:
echo $DISPLAY
xclock
fastqc
If the window does not appear, make sure XQuartz is running. If X11 is slow, use VNC instead.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
7.6 VNC access
VNC opens a full remote desktop. It is often more stable than X11 for graphical tools.






Use VNC only through SSH tunnel



Do not connect directly to a public VNC port. Start VNC on the server, create an SSH tunnel, then connect your VNC client to localhost.




7.6.1 Start VNC on the server
First, SSH to the server. Then run:
start-vnc
The first time, it asks you to create a VNC password.
Important:

this is a separate VNC password,
it is not your Linux password,
it is not your private key,
when asked for a view-only password, type n.

The command prints something like:
Display: :2
VNC port: 5902
Write down the VNC port. The port may be 5901, 5902, 5903, or another number.


7.6.2 Generic SSH tunnel
Open a new terminal window on your laptop and keep your original SSH session open. Replace 5902 with the port printed by start-vnc:
ssh -L 5902:localhost:5902 username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem
Keep this tunnel window open.
Then connect your VNC client to:
localhost:5902
If your VNC port is different, use that number instead.


7.6.3 Windows VNC with MobaXterm
In MobaXterm:

Start VNC on the server with start-vnc.
Open Tunneling > New SSH tunnel.
Choose local port forwarding.
Fill in:

Forwarded port: your VNC port
Remote server: localhost
Remote port: your VNC port
SSH server: SERVER_IP
SSH login: username
SSH port: 22
Use private key: key.pem
Example if start-vnc reports port 5902:
Forwarded port: 5902
Remote server: localhost
Remote port: 5902
SSH server: SERVER_IP
SSH login: username
SSH port: 22
Use private key: key.pem
Start the tunnel and keep it running.
Then create a VNC session:
Remote hostname: localhost
Port: your VNC port
Enter the VNC password you created with start-vnc.


7.6.4 macOS VNC
macOS includes Screen Sharing.

Start VNC on the server with start-vnc.
Open a new Terminal window and create the SSH tunnel:

ssh -L 5902:localhost:5902 username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem

In Finder, choose:

Go > Connect to Server

Enter:

vnc://localhost:5902

Enter the VNC password you created with start-vnc.

You can also use RealVNC Viewer or TigerVNC Viewer if Screen Sharing does not work well on your Mac.


7.6.5 Stop VNC when finished
List your VNC sessions:
list-vnc
If the display is :2, stop it with:
stop-vnc 2
If the display is :1, stop it with:
stop-vnc 1
If the remote desktop is locked, run:
fix-vnc-lock
list-vnc
Then restart your own VNC display.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
8.1 Windows PowerShell
python --version
pip --version
java -version
ssh -V

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
8.2 R console
R.version.string
sapply(c("dplyr", "ggplot2", "phyloseq", "vegan"), requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE)

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
8.3 macOS Terminal
python3 --version
R --version
java -version
ssh -V

If Python, R, SSH, and the R package check work, your laptop is ready for the core workshop activities.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 9.1 python is not recognized on Windows
text:
9.1 python is not recognized on Windows
Likely cause: Python was installed without adding it to PATH.
Simple fix:

Open the Python installer again.
Choose Modify or reinstall Python.
Tick Add python.exe to PATH if shown.
Close and reopen PowerShell.
Try:

python --version
Ask an instructor if Windows still cannot find Python.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
9.2 pip is not recognized
Likely cause: Windows can find Python but not the separate pip command.
Simple fix: use python -m pip instead:
python -m pip --version
python -m pip install numpy pandas
Ask for help if python -m pip also fails.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
9.3 R package installation fails
Likely causes:

internet connection problem,
CRAN mirror problem,
missing system build tools,
BiocManager has not been installed yet for Bioconductor packages,
or an older package binary is not available.

Simple fixes:

Try again with a different CRAN mirror.
Restart R or RStudio.
If the error mentions BiocManager, install it first:

if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("BiocManager")
}

On Windows, if the error mentions compilers, make, gcc, g++, gfortran, or Rtools, install Rtools45 from the official CRAN Rtools page:

https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/

On macOS, install Xcode Command Line Tools:

xcode-select --install
Ask an instructor if the error continues after these steps, especially if it mentions Fortran, gfortran, unavailable binary packages, or incompatible Bioconductor versions.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
9.4 phyloseq installation fails
Likely cause: phyloseq comes from Bioconductor, not ordinary CRAN, so it needs BiocManager.
Simple fix:
if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("BiocManager")
}

BiocManager::install("phyloseq", ask = FALSE, update = TRUE)
Ask an instructor if Bioconductor says your R version is not compatible.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
9.5 Windows says Rtools is required
Likely cause: R needs to build one or more packages from source.
Simple fix:

Install Rtools45 from the official CRAN page:

https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/

Keep the default installation folder:

C:\rtools45

Restart R or RStudio.
Check that R can find Rtools:

install.packages("pkgbuild")
pkgbuild::has_rtools(debug = TRUE)

Retry the failed package installation.

Ask an instructor for help if pkgbuild::has_rtools(debug = TRUE) does not return TRUE.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 9.6 macOS asks for command line tools
text:
9.6 macOS asks for command line tools
Likely cause: an R package needs to compile code.
Simple fix:
xcode-select --install
Wait for the installer to finish, then reopen R or RStudio.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 9.7 VS Code cannot connect to the server
text:
9.7 VS Code cannot connect to the server
Likely causes:

wrong server IP,
wrong username,
wrong key path,
key file was moved,
server not ready,
or local network blocks SSH.

Simple checks:

Try the same SSH command in PowerShell or Terminal.
Check that the key file exists at the path in your SSH config.
Check that HostName, User, and IdentityFile are correct.

Ask the admin if ordinary SSH fails too.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
9.8 SSH says Permission denied
Likely causes:

wrong username,
wrong private key,
private key not selected,
or the admin has not added your account yet.

Simple fix: check the command carefully:
ssh username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem
Ask the admin if the username, server IP, and key are correct but the error continues.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
9.9 SSH says Bad permissions
This is common on macOS when the key file is readable by other users.
Fix:
chmod 600 /path/to/key.pem
Then connect again.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
9.10 PEM key path contains spaces
On Windows, put quotation marks around the key path:
ssh username@SERVER_IP -i "C:\Users\Your Name\Downloads\microgen2026.pem"
On macOS, either quote the path or move the key to a simpler location:
ssh username@SERVER_IP -i "/Users/Your Name/Downloads/microgen2026.pem"

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
9.11 MobaXterm cannot open GUI windows
Likely causes:

the MobaXterm X server is not running,
X11 forwarding is not enabled,
or the remote program cannot find DISPLAY.

Simple checks inside the server SSH session:
echo $DISPLAY
xclock
If xclock does not open, use VNC or ask an instructor to check the MobaXterm session settings.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
9.12 X11 forwarding does not work
Likely causes:

XQuartz is not running on macOS,
VcXsrv is not running on Windows,
the SSH session was opened without -Y,
or network latency makes X11 unstable.

Simple fix on macOS:
ssh -Y username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem
If it remains unreliable, use VNC.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 9.13 VNC tunnel connects but the screen is blank
text:
9.13 VNC tunnel connects but the screen is blank
Likely causes:

you connected to the wrong port,
the tunnel window was closed,
VNC was not started on the server,
or the remote desktop is locked.

Simple checks on the server:
list-vnc
fix-vnc-lock
If needed, stop your display and start again:
stop-vnc 2
start-vnc
Replace 2 with your actual display number.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
9.14 Java is not found
Likely cause: Java was not installed or the terminal has not refreshed its PATH.
Simple fixes:

Install Java 17 from an official JDK provider.
Close and reopen PowerShell or Terminal.
Run:

java -version
Ask for help if Java is installed but still not found.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
9.15 RStudio cannot find R
Likely cause: RStudio was installed before R, or R installation did not finish.
Simple fixes:

Install R 4.5.0.
Restart the computer.
Open RStudio again.

If RStudio still cannot find R, ask an instructor to check the R installation path.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 10.1 Do I need to install Rtools45 before class?
text:
10.1 Do I need to install Rtools45 before class?
Usually no. Install Rtools45 only if you are on Windows and R reports that it needs to build a package from source.
You probably need Rtools45 if you see errors mentioning:

Rtools
make
gcc
g++
gfortran
compilation failed
package ... is only available in source form

If you use Windows with R 4.5.0, install Rtools45 from:
https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/
Keep the default location:
C:\rtools45
Then restart R or RStudio and retry the package installation.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 10.2 I installed Rtools45. Why does R still not find it?
text:
10.2 I installed Rtools45. Why does R still not find it?
Likely cause: R or RStudio was already open before Rtools45 was installed.
Simple fix:

Close all R, RGui, and RStudio windows.
Reopen R or RStudio.
Run:

install.packages("pkgbuild")
pkgbuild::has_rtools(debug = TRUE)
If it still does not return TRUE, check that Rtools45 was installed in C:\rtools45. Ask an instructor if your institution’s laptop security policy blocks the installer.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
10.3 Should macOS users install Rtools?
No. Rtools is for Windows only.
On macOS, if R needs to compile a package, install Apple’s Command Line Tools:
xcode-select --install
After the installer finishes, close and reopen R or RStudio.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 10.4 R says there is no package called 'BiocManager'. What should I do?
text:
10.4 R says there is no package called 'BiocManager'. What should I do?
Install BiocManager first:
install.packages("BiocManager")
Then install the Bioconductor package:
BiocManager::install("phyloseq", ask = FALSE, update = TRUE)
BiocManager is not included with a fresh R installation, so this message is normal on a new computer.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 10.5 R asks Do you want to install from sources the package which needs compilation?
text:
10.5 R asks Do you want to install from sources the package which needs compilation?
For beginners, choose No if R also offers a binary package. Binary packages are easier to install.
If R cannot offer a binary package and says source installation is required:

Windows: install Rtools45, restart R or RStudio, and try again.
macOS: install Xcode Command Line Tools, restart R or RStudio, and try again.

Ask an instructor if the package is still failing after the build tools are installed.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 10.6 R asks me to update many packages. Should I say yes?
text:
10.6 R asks me to update many packages. Should I say yes?
Usually yes, but it can take time. If you are in the workshop room and the internet is slow, ask an instructor first.
For the phyloseq command in this guide, we use:
BiocManager::install("phyloseq", ask = FALSE, update = TRUE)
This allows Bioconductor to update packages needed for compatibility.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 10.7 I see package is not available for this version of R.
text:
10.7 I see package is not available for this version of R.
Likely causes:

you installed the wrong R version,
the package repository was temporarily unavailable,
or R is using a stale package mirror.

Simple checks:
R.version.string
You should see R 4.5.0 for this workshop. If the version is different, ask an instructor whether to reinstall R 4.5.0 or continue with your current version.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 10.8 Python installed, but python --version does not work on Windows.
text:
10.8 Python installed, but python --version does not work on Windows.
Likely cause: Python was installed without adding it to PATH.
Simple fix:

Re-run the Python installer.
Choose Modify or reinstall.
Tick Add python.exe to PATH.
Close and reopen PowerShell.
Run:

python --version
If that still fails, try:
py --version

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
text:
10.9 pip install numpy pandas fails.
Use Python’s module form of pip, which is more reliable:
python -m pip install --upgrade pip
python -m pip install numpy pandas
On macOS:
python3 -m pip install --upgrade pip
python3 -m pip install numpy pandas
If the error mentions internet access, try a different network or ask an instructor.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 10.10 VS Code Remote SSH asks me to choose Linux, Windows, or macOS for the server.
text:
10.10 VS Code Remote SSH asks me to choose Linux, Windows, or macOS for the server.
Choose Linux. The workshop server is a Linux server.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 10.11 VS Code Remote SSH keeps asking for a password.
text:
10.11 VS Code Remote SSH keeps asking for a password.
Likely causes:

VS Code cannot find your .pem key file,
the key path in the SSH config is wrong,
or the server username is wrong.

Open your SSH config and check:
Host microgen2026
    HostName SERVER_IP
    User username
    IdentityFile /path/to/key.pem
On Windows, a key path may look like:
IdentityFile C:\Users\YourName\Downloads\microgen2026.pem
Ask the admin if the username, server IP, and key path are all correct but login still fails.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 10.12 SSH says WARNING: UNPROTECTED PRIVATE KEY FILE.
text:
10.12 SSH says WARNING: UNPROTECTED PRIVATE KEY FILE.
This usually happens on macOS or Linux when the .pem file permissions are too open.
Run:
chmod 600 /path/to/key.pem
Then try SSH again.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 10.13 MobaXterm opens SSH, but graphical windows do not appear.
text:
10.13 MobaXterm opens SSH, but graphical windows do not appear.
Check X11 first:
echo $DISPLAY
xclock
If xclock does not appear, check that MobaXterm’s X server is running and that X11 forwarding is enabled in the session settings.
If X11 remains slow or unreliable, use VNC instead.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 10.14 VNC asks for a password. Is that my server password?
text:
10.14 VNC asks for a password. Is that my server password?
No. It is the VNC password you created when you ran:
start-vnc
It is separate from your .pem key and separate from any Linux password.
If you forgot the VNC password, ask an instructor before resetting it.

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 10.15 The VNC port is not 5901. Is that a problem?
text:
10.15 The VNC port is not 5901. Is that a problem?
No. Use the port printed by start-vnc, for example 5902 or 5903.
If start-vnc prints:
VNC port: 5902
then your tunnel should use 5902:
ssh -L 5902:localhost:5902 username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem

title: 0.1 Setup your workspace
section: 10.16 Java is optional. Should I install it now?
text:
10.16 Java is optional. Should I install it now?
Install Java only if your instructor asks you to use Artemis or another Java-based graphical tool.
For this workshop, Java 17 is a good default. After installing, check:
java -version
If you are short on time before class, prioritize R, Python, VS Code, and server access first.

title: 0.1.1 Windows 10/11 setup
text:
Open the official VS Code download page: https://code.visualstudio.com/download
Under Windows, download either:

User Installer x64 if you are using your own laptop and do not have administrator rights, or
System Installer x64 if your administrator or IT team prefers system-wide installation.

Run the installer.
When the installer shows optional tasks, tick these options if available:

Add to PATH
Add “Open with Code” action
Register Code as an editor for supported file types

Finish the installation and open VS Code.

In VS Code, open the Extensions view and install:

Remote - SSH by Microsoft
Python by Microsoft
R by REditorSupport
Quarto by Quarto




VS Code Extensions: Remote - SSH, Python, R, and Quarto








Recommended shortcut: import the MicroGen2026 VS Code profile



After installing VS Code, you can download the workshop profile file: Microgen2026.code-profile.
To import it, open VS Code and choose Manage > Profiles > Profiles > More Actions… (dropdown icon) > Import Profile…, then select the downloaded Microgen2026.code-profile file.
If the profile imports successfully, it replaces the manual VS Code extension setup above and adds workshop-specific settings/customizations. If the profile import does not work on your computer, install the extensions manually using the list above.



VS Code menu for importing a profile










What this does



VS Code is the main editor for the workshop. The Remote - SSH extension lets VS Code open your assigned server workspace as if it were a local folder.






Open the official Python 3.13 download page: https://www.python.org/downloads/latest/python3.13/
Download the Windows installer (64-bit).
Run the installer.
On the first installer screen, tick Add python.exe to PATH.
Choose Install Now.




Python installer screen showing Add python.exe to PATH








Important Windows step



The Add python.exe to PATH checkbox is important. It lets Windows find Python when you type python in PowerShell.


Open PowerShell from the Start menu and check Python:
python --version
pip --version
You should see Python 3.13 and a pip version. pip is the tool Python uses to install Python packages.
Update pip:
python -m pip install --upgrade pip
This asks Python to update its package installer.
Install the required Python packages:
python -m pip install numpy pandas
This installs numpy and pandas, which are common Python packages for numerical and table-based data work.
Verify the packages:
python -c "import numpy, pandas; print('Python packages OK')"
If the packages are installed correctly, PowerShell prints Python packages OK.



R 4.5.0 is the R version used by the Day 4 practical materials. It is no longer the newest R release, so use the official CRAN old-release page for Windows.

Open the official CRAN Windows R 4.5.0 page: https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/base/old/4.5.0/
Download R-4.5.0-win.exe.
Run the installer.
Accept the default options unless your instructor tells you otherwise.

Open R or RGui from the Start menu and run:
R.version.string
You should see a line beginning with R version 4.5.0.



Most Windows users do not need Rtools at the start. R normally installs pre-built Windows package binaries from CRAN and Bioconductor.
Rtools is needed only when R says it must install a package from source or when an error mentions compilers, make, gcc, g++, gfortran, or Rtools.
For R 4.5.0 on Windows, use Rtools45.






Do not install a different Rtools version



Rtools must match your R version. For this workshop, Windows participants installing R 4.5.0 should use Rtools45, not Rtools44, Rtools43, or older versions.



Open the official CRAN Rtools page: https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/
Choose RTools 4.5.
Download the Rtools45 installer.
Run the installer.
Keep the default installation location, usually:

C:\rtools45






Beginner recommendation



If your R package installation works, you can skip Rtools. If installation fails with a compiler-related message, install Rtools45 and then retry the R package command.


After installing Rtools45, open a fresh R or RStudio session and run:
install.packages("pkgbuild")
pkgbuild::has_rtools(debug = TRUE)
If Rtools is detected, this should return TRUE.



RStudio is optional. It is helpful if you prefer a graphical interface for R, but VS Code is enough for most workshop tasks.

Open the official Posit download page: https://posit.co/downloads/
Choose RStudio Desktop.
Download the Windows installer.
Run the installer with the default settings.
Open RStudio and check that it starts an R console.







RStudio cannot replace R



RStudio is an editor. R is the programming language. Install R first, then install RStudio.





Open R, RGui, or the RStudio Console. Paste this code and press Enter:
install.packages(c(
  "tidyverse",
  "dplyr",
  "tidyr",
  "readr",
  "ggplot2",
  "ggrepel",
  "vegan",
  "patchwork",
  "scales",
  "data.table",
  "broom",
  "knitr"
))
This installs the packages that come from CRAN.
Now install BiocManager if it is not already installed:
if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("BiocManager")
}
BiocManager is the installer used for Bioconductor packages. Many learners will not have it yet.
Then install phyloseq:
BiocManager::install("phyloseq", ask = FALSE, update = TRUE)
phyloseq comes from Bioconductor, so it is installed after BiocManager is available.






If R asks about a CRAN mirror



Choose a mirror close to you, or choose the cloud mirror if it is listed. Any official CRAN mirror should work.


Verify the R packages:
packages <- c(
  "dplyr", "tidyr", "readr", "ggplot2", "ggrepel",
  "phyloseq", "vegan", "patchwork", "scales",
  "data.table", "broom", "knitr"
)

sapply(packages, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE)
Each package should return TRUE. If one returns FALSE, copy the package name and ask an instructor for help.






If R asks to install from source on Windows



Choose the binary version if R offers one. If R says a package must be installed from source, you may need Rtools45. Install Rtools45 from the section above, restart R or RStudio, and run the package installation again.





MobaXterm is recommended for Windows participants who need SSH, X11 forwarding, SFTP file browsing, or VNC tunneling. The portable edition does not require administrator installation.

Open the official MobaXterm Home Edition page: https://mobaxterm.mobatek.net/download-home-edition.html
Download the Portable edition.
Extract the ZIP file to a folder you can find later, such as Downloads\MobaXterm.
Open the extracted folder.
Run MobaXterm_Personal_*.exe.




MobaXterm portable folder showing MobaXterm_Personal executable





Open MobaXterm.
Click Session.
Choose SSH.
In Remote host, enter the server IP address given by the admin.
Tick Specify username and enter your username.
Open Advanced SSH settings.
Tick Use private key and select your .pem file.
Make sure X11-forwarding is enabled.
Click OK to connect.
Save the session if MobaXterm asks.







Keep the key file path simple



If possible, store your .pem key in a folder path without spaces, for example C:\Users\YourName\Downloads\microgen2026.pem. If the path contains spaces, keep quotation marks around it when using PowerShell.






Java is optional for most participants. It is mainly needed for Artemis.
Use OpenJDK 17 unless an instructor gives you a different version. On Windows, the recommended option for this guide is Eclipse Temurin OpenJDK 17 LTS.

Open the Eclipse Temurin OpenJDK 17 installation page: https://adoptium.net/installation/?variant=openjdk17
Choose Windows.
Download the Temurin 17 JDK MSI installer for your computer, usually x64.
Run the installer.
Keep the default options. If the installer offers PATH or JAVA_HOME options, enable them.







Why OpenJDK 17?



OpenJDK 17 is a long-term support Java version and is a good default for Java-based teaching tools such as Artemis. Other OpenJDK 17 builds, such as Microsoft Build of OpenJDK 17, are acceptable if your institution already uses them, but this guide mainly refers to Temurin OpenJDK 17.


After installation, open PowerShell:
java -version
You should see a Java version number.

title: 0.1.1 Windows 10/11 setup
text:
Open the official VS Code download page: https://code.visualstudio.com/download
Under Windows, download either:

User Installer x64 if you are using your own laptop and do not have administrator rights, or
System Installer x64 if your administrator or IT team prefers system-wide installation.

Run the installer.
When the installer shows optional tasks, tick these options if available:

Add to PATH
Add “Open with Code” action
Register Code as an editor for supported file types

Finish the installation and open VS Code.

In VS Code, open the Extensions view and install:

Remote - SSH by Microsoft
Python by Microsoft
R by REditorSupport
Quarto by Quarto




VS Code Extensions: Remote - SSH, Python, R, and Quarto








Recommended shortcut: import the MicroGen2026 VS Code profile



After installing VS Code, you can download the workshop profile file: Microgen2026.code-profile.
To import it, open VS Code and choose Manage > Profiles > Profiles > More Actions… (dropdown icon) > Import Profile…, then select the downloaded Microgen2026.code-profile file.
If the profile imports successfully, it replaces the manual VS Code extension setup above and adds workshop-specific settings/customizations. If the profile import does not work on your computer, install the extensions manually using the list above.



VS Code menu for importing a profile










What this does



VS Code is the main editor for the workshop. The Remote - SSH extension lets VS Code open your assigned server workspace as if it were a local folder.

title: 0.1.1 Windows 10/11 setup
text:
Open the official Python 3.13 download page: https://www.python.org/downloads/latest/python3.13/
Download the Windows installer (64-bit).
Run the installer.
On the first installer screen, tick Add python.exe to PATH.
Choose Install Now.




Python installer screen showing Add python.exe to PATH








Important Windows step



The Add python.exe to PATH checkbox is important. It lets Windows find Python when you type python in PowerShell.


Open PowerShell from the Start menu and check Python:
python --version
pip --version
You should see Python 3.13 and a pip version. pip is the tool Python uses to install Python packages.
Update pip:
python -m pip install --upgrade pip
This asks Python to update its package installer.
Install the required Python packages:
python -m pip install numpy pandas
This installs numpy and pandas, which are common Python packages for numerical and table-based data work.
Verify the packages:
python -c "import numpy, pandas; print('Python packages OK')"
If the packages are installed correctly, PowerShell prints Python packages OK.

title: 0.1.1 Windows 10/11 setup
text:
R 4.5.0 is the R version used by the Day 4 practical materials. It is no longer the newest R release, so use the official CRAN old-release page for Windows.

Open the official CRAN Windows R 4.5.0 page: https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/base/old/4.5.0/
Download R-4.5.0-win.exe.
Run the installer.
Accept the default options unless your instructor tells you otherwise.

Open R or RGui from the Start menu and run:
R.version.string
You should see a line beginning with R version 4.5.0.

title: 0.1.1 Windows 10/11 setup
text:
Most Windows users do not need Rtools at the start. R normally installs pre-built Windows package binaries from CRAN and Bioconductor.
Rtools is needed only when R says it must install a package from source or when an error mentions compilers, make, gcc, g++, gfortran, or Rtools.
For R 4.5.0 on Windows, use Rtools45.






Do not install a different Rtools version



Rtools must match your R version. For this workshop, Windows participants installing R 4.5.0 should use Rtools45, not Rtools44, Rtools43, or older versions.



Open the official CRAN Rtools page: https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/
Choose RTools 4.5.
Download the Rtools45 installer.
Run the installer.
Keep the default installation location, usually:

C:\rtools45






Beginner recommendation



If your R package installation works, you can skip Rtools. If installation fails with a compiler-related message, install Rtools45 and then retry the R package command.


After installing Rtools45, open a fresh R or RStudio session and run:
install.packages("pkgbuild")
pkgbuild::has_rtools(debug = TRUE)
If Rtools is detected, this should return TRUE.

title: 0.1.1 Windows 10/11 setup
text:
RStudio is optional. It is helpful if you prefer a graphical interface for R, but VS Code is enough for most workshop tasks.

Open the official Posit download page: https://posit.co/downloads/
Choose RStudio Desktop.
Download the Windows installer.
Run the installer with the default settings.
Open RStudio and check that it starts an R console.







RStudio cannot replace R



RStudio is an editor. R is the programming language. Install R first, then install RStudio.

title: 0.1.1 Windows 10/11 setup
text:
Open R, RGui, or the RStudio Console. Paste this code and press Enter:
install.packages(c(
  "tidyverse",
  "dplyr",
  "tidyr",
  "readr",
  "ggplot2",
  "ggrepel",
  "vegan",
  "patchwork",
  "scales",
  "data.table",
  "broom",
  "knitr"
))
This installs the packages that come from CRAN.
Now install BiocManager if it is not already installed:
if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("BiocManager")
}
BiocManager is the installer used for Bioconductor packages. Many learners will not have it yet.
Then install phyloseq:
BiocManager::install("phyloseq", ask = FALSE, update = TRUE)
phyloseq comes from Bioconductor, so it is installed after BiocManager is available.






If R asks about a CRAN mirror



Choose a mirror close to you, or choose the cloud mirror if it is listed. Any official CRAN mirror should work.


Verify the R packages:
packages <- c(
  "dplyr", "tidyr", "readr", "ggplot2", "ggrepel",
  "phyloseq", "vegan", "patchwork", "scales",
  "data.table", "broom", "knitr"
)

sapply(packages, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE)
Each package should return TRUE. If one returns FALSE, copy the package name and ask an instructor for help.






If R asks to install from source on Windows



Choose the binary version if R offers one. If R says a package must be installed from source, you may need Rtools45. Install Rtools45 from the section above, restart R or RStudio, and run the package installation again.

title: 0.1.1 Windows 10/11 setup
text:
MobaXterm is recommended for Windows participants who need SSH, X11 forwarding, SFTP file browsing, or VNC tunneling. The portable edition does not require administrator installation.

Open the official MobaXterm Home Edition page: https://mobaxterm.mobatek.net/download-home-edition.html
Download the Portable edition.
Extract the ZIP file to a folder you can find later, such as Downloads\MobaXterm.
Open the extracted folder.
Run MobaXterm_Personal_*.exe.




MobaXterm portable folder showing MobaXterm_Personal executable





Open MobaXterm.
Click Session.
Choose SSH.
In Remote host, enter the server IP address given by the admin.
Tick Specify username and enter your username.
Open Advanced SSH settings.
Tick Use private key and select your .pem file.
Make sure X11-forwarding is enabled.
Click OK to connect.
Save the session if MobaXterm asks.







Keep the key file path simple



If possible, store your .pem key in a folder path without spaces, for example C:\Users\YourName\Downloads\microgen2026.pem. If the path contains spaces, keep quotation marks around it when using PowerShell.

title: 0.1.1 Windows 10/11 setup
text:
Java is optional for most participants. It is mainly needed for Artemis.
Use OpenJDK 17 unless an instructor gives you a different version. On Windows, the recommended option for this guide is Eclipse Temurin OpenJDK 17 LTS.

Open the Eclipse Temurin OpenJDK 17 installation page: https://adoptium.net/installation/?variant=openjdk17
Choose Windows.
Download the Temurin 17 JDK MSI installer for your computer, usually x64.
Run the installer.
Keep the default options. If the installer offers PATH or JAVA_HOME options, enable them.







Why OpenJDK 17?



OpenJDK 17 is a long-term support Java version and is a good default for Java-based teaching tools such as Artemis. Other OpenJDK 17 builds, such as Microsoft Build of OpenJDK 17, are acceptable if your institution already uses them, but this guide mainly refers to Temurin OpenJDK 17.


After installation, open PowerShell:
java -version
You should see a Java version number.

title: 0.1.2 macOS setup
text:
Open the Apple menu in the top-left corner.
Choose About This Mac.
Look for Chip or Processor.

Use this rule:

Processor: Intel means Intel Mac.
Chip: Apple M1/M2/M3/M4 means Apple Silicon Mac.





Open the official VS Code download page: https://code.visualstudio.com/download
Under Mac, choose:

Intel chip for Intel Macs,
Apple silicon for M1/M2/M3/M4 Macs,
or Universal if you are unsure.

Open the downloaded .dmg file.
Drag Visual Studio Code to the Applications folder.
Open VS Code from Applications.

Install these VS Code extensions:

Remote - SSH by Microsoft
Python by Microsoft
R by REditorSupport
Quarto by Quarto







Recommended shortcut: import the MicroGen2026 VS Code profile



After installing VS Code, you can download the workshop profile file: Microgen2026.code-profile.
To import it, open VS Code and choose Manage > Profiles > Profiles > More Actions… (dropdown icon) > Import Profile…, then select the downloaded Microgen2026.code-profile file.
If the profile imports successfully, it replaces the manual VS Code extension setup above and adds workshop-specific settings/customizations. If the profile import does not work on your computer, install the extensions manually using the list above.



VS Code menu for importing a profile







There are two acceptable routes. Use Route A if you are not comfortable with Terminal.



Open the official Python 3.13 download page: https://www.python.org/downloads/latest/python3.13/
Download the macOS installer.
Open the .pkg installer.
Follow the default installation steps.

Open Terminal from Applications > Utilities and check Python:
python3 --version
python3 -m pip --version



Use this only if you already use Homebrew or are comfortable with Terminal.
Install Python 3.13:
brew install python@3.13
This asks Homebrew to install Python 3.13.
Check Python:
python3 --version
python3 -m pip --version
Install Python packages:
python3 -m pip install --upgrade pip
python3 -m pip install numpy pandas
The first command updates the Python package installer. The second installs numpy and pandas.
Verify:
python3 -c "import numpy, pandas; print('Python packages OK')"




R 4.5.0 is now an older R release. CRAN still provides the official installers in architecture-specific macOS directories.



Open the CRAN Intel macOS base directory: https://cran.r-project.org/bin/macosx/big-sur-x86_64/base/
Download R-4.5.0-x86_64.pkg.
Open the .pkg file and accept the default installation options.





Open the CRAN Apple Silicon macOS base directory: https://cran.r-project.org/bin/macosx/big-sur-arm64/base/
Download R-4.5.0-arm64.pkg.
Open the .pkg file and accept the default installation options.







macOS version note



The R 4.5.0 macOS packages were built for modern macOS versions. If your Mac is older or cannot install the package, ask an instructor before installing a different R version.


Verify in Terminal:
R --version
Or open R.app and run:
R.version.string
You should see R 4.5.0.





Open the official Posit download page: https://posit.co/downloads/
Download RStudio Desktop for macOS.
Open the downloaded file.
Drag RStudio to Applications if prompted.
Open RStudio and confirm that it detects R.




Open R.app or RStudio Console. Paste:
install.packages(c(
  "tidyverse",
  "dplyr",
  "tidyr",
  "readr",
  "ggplot2",
  "ggrepel",
  "vegan",
  "patchwork",
  "scales",
  "data.table",
  "broom",
  "knitr"
))
This installs the packages that come from CRAN.
Now install BiocManager if it is not already installed:
if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("BiocManager")
}
BiocManager is the installer used for Bioconductor packages. Many learners will not have it yet.
Then install phyloseq:
BiocManager::install("phyloseq", ask = FALSE, update = TRUE)
phyloseq comes from Bioconductor, so it is installed after BiocManager is available.
Verify:
packages <- c(
  "dplyr", "tidyr", "readr", "ggplot2", "ggrepel",
  "phyloseq", "vegan", "patchwork", "scales",
  "data.table", "broom", "knitr"
)

sapply(packages, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE)
If R asks whether to install from source, choose the binary option if it is available.






Rtools is not used on macOS



Rtools is for Windows only. On macOS, packages that need compilation use Apple’s Command Line Tools instead.


If compilation fails on macOS, install the Xcode Command Line Tools:
xcode-select --install
This opens an Apple installer for command-line build tools. After it finishes, reopen R or RStudio and try the package installation again.



Java is optional. It is mainly needed for Artemis.
Use OpenJDK 17 unless an instructor gives you a different version. The recommended option for this guide is Eclipse Temurin OpenJDK 17 LTS.

Open the Eclipse Temurin OpenJDK 17 installation page: https://adoptium.net/installation/?variant=openjdk17
Choose macOS.
Download the Temurin 17 JDK package for your Mac:

Intel Mac: macOS x64
Apple Silicon Mac: macOS aarch64 or Apple Silicon

Open the .pkg installer and follow the default steps.

Other official OpenJDK 17 builds, such as Microsoft Build of OpenJDK 17, are acceptable if your institution already uses them.
Verify in Terminal:
java -version

text:

Use OpenJDK 17 unless an instructor gives you a different version. The recommended option for this guide is Eclipse Temurin OpenJDK 17 LTS.

Open the Eclipse Temurin OpenJDK 17 installation page: https://adoptium.net/installation/?variant=openjdk17
Choose macOS.
Download the Temurin 17 JDK package for your Mac:

Intel Mac: macOS x64
Apple Silicon Mac: macOS aarch64 or Apple Silicon

Open the .pkg installer and follow the default steps.

Verify in Terminal:
java -version

title: 0.1.3 Ubuntu setup
text:
Use this section if you are setting up your own Ubuntu laptop, WSL with graphical support, or an Ubuntu virtual machine.






Warning



Do not install system packages on the workshop server or course instance unless an instructor or admin explicitly tells you to. The workshop server is shared and already has the required teaching environment prepared.




The official VS Code documentation recommends downloading the .deb package for Debian/Ubuntu systems from https://code.visualstudio.com/download.

Download the .deb package for Ubuntu.
Open a terminal in the folder where the file was downloaded.
Install it with:

sudo apt install ./code_*.deb
Open VS Code and install these extensions:

Remote - SSH by Microsoft
Python by Microsoft
R by REditorSupport
Quarto by Quarto







Recommended shortcut: import the MicroGen2026 VS Code profile



After installing VS Code, you can download the workshop profile file: Microgen2026.code-profile.
To import it, open VS Code and choose Manage > Profiles > Profiles > More Actions… (dropdown icon) > Import Profile…, then select the downloaded Microgen2026.code-profile file.
If the profile imports successfully, it replaces the manual VS Code extension setup above and adds workshop-specific settings/customizations. If the profile import does not work on your computer, install the extensions manually using the list above.



VS Code menu for importing a profile







Ubuntu may provide a Python version through apt, but the workshop target is Python 3.13. If your Ubuntu release does not provide Python 3.13 directly, use the official Python source release or ask an instructor before class.
For a simple local setup, install Python tools and then check the version:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install python3 python3-pip python3-venv
python3 --version
python3 -m pip --version
Install the Python packages:
python3 -m pip install --upgrade pip
python3 -m pip install numpy pandas
python3 -c "import numpy, pandas; print('Python packages OK')"



CRAN provides Ubuntu packages for the current R 4.5 release series on supported Ubuntu LTS releases. Follow the official CRAN Ubuntu page if these commands need updating: https://cran.r-project.org/bin/linux/ubuntu/.
sudo apt update -qq
sudo apt install --no-install-recommends software-properties-common dirmngr wget ca-certificates
wget -qO- https://cloud.r-project.org/bin/linux/ubuntu/marutter_pubkey.asc | sudo tee -a /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/cran_ubuntu_key.asc
sudo add-apt-repository "deb https://cloud.r-project.org/bin/linux/ubuntu $(lsb_release -cs)-cran40/"
sudo apt install --no-install-recommends r-base r-base-dev
R --version
Install the required R packages from inside R:
install.packages(c(
  "tidyverse",
  "dplyr",
  "tidyr",
  "readr",
  "ggplot2",
  "ggrepel",
  "vegan",
  "patchwork",
  "scales",
  "data.table",
  "broom",
  "knitr",
  "BiocManager"
))

BiocManager::install("phyloseq")



sudo apt install openjdk-17-jdk
java -version
The remaining Ubuntu commands below are mainly for optional graphical applications.
Use this section only if you are setting up an Ubuntu laptop, WSL with graphical support, or an Ubuntu virtual machine. These commands require administrator access through sudo.






Workshop server note



Learners usually should not install system packages on the workshop server. Use this Ubuntu section for your own Ubuntu computer or VM unless an admin tells you otherwise.


Update the package index:
sudo apt update
Install OpenJDK 17, Artemis, NCBI BLAST+, and SeaView from Ubuntu packages:
sudo apt install openjdk-17-jdk artemis ncbi-blast+ seaview
Check the tools:
java -version
art
blastn -version
makeblastdb -version
seaview
If art does not start Artemis on your Ubuntu version, try:
artemis
Install BandageNG with a conda-compatible package manager if you use conda, mamba, micromamba, or pixi:
conda create -n bandageng -c conda-forge -c bioconda bandage_ng
conda activate bandageng
BandageNG
If the executable name is different in your environment, list matching commands:
compgen -c | grep -i bandage






Ubuntu GUI requirement



Artemis, BandageNG, and SeaView are graphical apps. They need a desktop session, X11, VNC, or another working display system. On a headless server, install and use them only if your instructor or admin has configured graphical access.
